languages._comment_docker_repo: Official docker.io/library/rust images. Old minors <=1.54 are Debian stretch/buster-based (buster confirmed through 1.54, bullseye confirmed starting exactly at 1.55 -- verified live via `docker run rust:X.Y-slim cat /etc/os-release` across the whole transition window, not estimated) and need the same archive.debian.org mirror fix already used elsewhere in this project for EOL Debian codenames.

versions.notes: 1.0-1.30 predate this project's confirmed Docker image floor (rust:1.31-slim, 2018-12-06, is the oldest pullable official tag -- verified live via `docker manifest inspect`). No image ever existed for these historically real compiler versions in this registry.
Kept in sparse coverage: oldest tracked rustc (Docker floor) + edition 2018 + lowest MSRV floor (Rocket 0.4/Iron 0.6/warp 0.1/ring 0.16/bcrypt 0.5+0.10/argon2 0.1+0.3/rust-crypto 0.2)

Excluded from this registry's sparse language-version coverage (rebuilt 2026-07-24, by explicit user decision): no framework or cryptography-library bucket in this registry has a compatibility floor at this exact minor, and it is not a Rust edition or Debian codename transition point. Every (framework, library) pairing already has a working rust_ver among the included set -- for any pair, the required combined floor is max(fw_floor, lib_floor), which is always one of the two floors themselves, both already covered. Unlike framework/library include/exclude (editable via the dashboard's version_overrides table), language versions are NOT dashboard-editable by deliberate design -- if a future addition needs this exact version, it must be included here manually.

Excluded from this registry's sparse language-version coverage (rebuilt 2026-07-24, by explicit user decision): no framework or cryptography-library bucket in this registry has a compatibility floor at this exact minor, and it is not a Rust edition or Debian codename transition point. frameworks.name: Rocket

version.notes: Nightly-only, same API era as 0.4 (unstable proc_macro_hygiene/decl_macro feature gates) -- not separately build-tested; superseded by the 0.4 bucket below. Date confirmed via crates.io.
Nightly-only, same API era as 0.4 -- not separately build-tested. Date approximate (interpolated from 0.1/0.3 spacing), not independently pinned.
Nightly-only, same API era as 0.4 -- not separately build-tested. Date approximate (interpolated), not independently pinned.

Nightly-ONLY for its entire lifespan (confirmed: needs #![feature(proc_macro_hygiene, decl_macro)], features never stabilized in the form Rocket used -- no stable Rust ever worked, not just "old stable"). Resolves to latest patch 0.4.11 (2022-05-26). ARCHITECTURAL NOTE: since this needs a pinned nightly toolchain regardless of which stable rustc is selected as the "language version", the Dockerfile for this framework major installs a fixed nightly via rustup independent of the base image's own stable toolchain -- the language-version axis is nominal/cosmetic for this one framework, unlike every other combo in this registry. Compatibility is intentionally the full range rather than a real MSRV-derived floor, since any stable base can host the pinned nightly on top.
First stable-Rust, async version -- genuine rewrite, confirmed MSRV 1.64 via crates.io rust-version field. Resolves to 0.5.1 (2024-05-23).

frameworks.notes: Confirmed via crates.io + GitHub release notes: Rocket has exactly TWO real API eras, not a gradual evolution -- 0.1-0.4 (nightly-only, sync handlers, unstable macro features) and 0.5+ (stable Rust, async, MSRV 1.64). See the 0.4 entry's notes for how this project handles the nightly-toolchain requirement in the generator.

frameworks.name: actix-web

version.notes: Early pre-tokio-1.0 era -- not separately build-tested, superseded by the confirmed 3.x/4.x baseline below. Date is actix-web's first crates.io release.
Early pre-tokio-1.0 era -- not separately build-tested. Date approximate (interpolated between 1.x and 3.x), not independently pinned.
MSRV 1.42, confirmed via actix-web's own v3 announcement wiki page. Resolves to 3.3.3 (2021-12-18, last 3.x release, old actix-rt/tokio-0.2).

Rewrite onto tokio-1 + new extractor API -- genuine breaking era boundary, confirmed via MIGRATION-4.0.md. MSRV 1.54 AT 4.0.0 specifically (confirmed via crates.io); 4.x's MSRV has climbed considerably since for later patches (into the 1.75+ range) -- this registry's own "resolve to latest patch" convention means the true floor for whatever patch actually gets resolved may be higher than 1.54. Flagged for a real-build spot-check across the range, same as any other approximated floor in this registry.

frameworks.notes: Confirmed via crates.io + MIGRATION-4.0.md: the 3.x->4.x jump is a real, documented MSRV-raising rewrite (tokio 0.2->1.0). 1.x/2.x predate a confirmed stable baseline and are tracked as reference-only.

frameworks.name: axum

version.notes: Pre-http-crate-1.0 era. Release date approximate (not independently pinned via crates.io in this pass); MSRV floor (1.63) is an era-based ESTIMATE, not a confirmed rust-version field -- axum's MSRV field convention only became consistent later. Flag for real-build verification.

http crate 0.2->1.0 + hyper 0.14->1.0 -- the single biggest breaking change in axum's history, confirmed via the first research pass. MSRV floor (1.70) is an era-based ESTIMATE pending real-build confirmation; current axum MSRV is 1.80 but that's for the LATEST 0.8.x, not 0.7.0 itself.

frameworks.notes: axum's own API shape (extractor-based) stays fairly consistent release to release -- confirmed via research this is the WEAKEST multi-era framework in this registry, included for density/popularity (397M downloads) rather than a sharp API break like Rocket's.

frameworks.name: warp

version.notes: Confirmed release date via crates.io. warp has NEVER declared an MSRV (confirmed: no version 0.1-0.4 has a rust-version field) -- compatibility floor/ceiling here is an ESTIMATE based on the release's contemporary rustc era, not a documented constraint. Verify via real build.
Confirmed release date via crates.io. No declared MSRV -- floor estimated from era (post async/await stabilization at 1.39). Verify via real build.
Confirmed release date via crates.io. No declared MSRV -- floor estimated from era. Verify via real build.
Confirmed release date via crates.io. No declared MSRV -- floor estimated from era. Verify via real build.

frameworks.notes: Confirmed via crates.io API: warp has 4 genuinely distinct release lines (2018/2020/2021/2025) but has never once declared a rust-version field across its entire history -- every compatibility floor/ceiling above is this project's own era-based estimate, not a spec, and should be treated as lower-confidence than the MSRV-sourced numbers elsewhere in this registry until spot-checked with real builds.

frameworks.name: Iron

version.notes: Final release (0.6.1), confirmed via crates.io. Officially unmaintained since. Sync, pre-async, middleware-chain Handler trait -- a genuine legacy baseline, directly analogous to this project's oldest tracked majors in other languages (e.g. PHP's Slim 1). No MSRV ever declared and no `edition` field in its Cargo.toml (implies edition 2015) -- compatibility is the FULL range as a conservative estimate (old, simple, sync code plausibly still compiles on modern rustc, but this has NOT been verified with a real build yet).

frameworks.notes: Single-era legacy framework, included specifically as the pre-async baseline (mirrors this registry's treatment of Iron's real historical role) rather than for version density.

cryptography_libs.name: ring

version.notes: Resolves to latest 0.16.x. MSRV for the 0.16 line was NOT independently confirmed in this pass (GitHub Cargo.toml fetch failed) -- floor is an era-based estimate (project floor), verify via real build.

MSRV 1.61.0, confirmed via crates.io rust-version field on 0.17.8. Resolves to 0.17.14 (2025-03-11). Hybrid crate -- vendors BoringSSL's C/asm code, not pure Rust, not a system-library binding either (confirmed via ring's own README).

cryptography_libs.notes: General-purpose crate (hashing/AEAD/signing in one package, closest analog to PyNaCl's scope in this registry's other languages). RUSTSEC-2025-0007 ("ring is unmaintained") was issued 2025-02-21 during a brief maintainer hiatus and has since been WITHDRAWN -- confirmed via direct research; treat ring as actively maintained, not abandoned.

cryptography_libs.name: rsa

version.notes: VULNERABLE to the Marvin Attack timing side-channel (RUSTSEC-2023-0071) -- confirmed unpatched at 0.8.2 and for the entire 0.9.0-0.9.6 line (through Dec 2023). Deliberately tracked pre-fix, mirroring this registry's existing pattern of tracking real historical CVE-affected versions (e.g. phpseclib 1.0.30 in PHP). Compatibility floor is an era-based estimate (no rust-version field confirmed at this version), verify via real build.

Resolves to latest 0.9.x. MSRV 1.65 confirmed via crates.io rust-version field at 0.9.0. The Marvin Attack fix landed at 0.9.7 (2024-11-26, ~20 months after the CVE was known) -- resolving to "latest" means this bucket represents the PATCHED state, contrasting with the 0.8 bucket above.

cryptography_libs.notes: RustCrypto pure-Rust RSA implementation. The 0.8 vs 0.9(latest) buckets deliberately capture a real, dated vulnerable-vs-fixed pair (RUSTSEC-2023-0071 / the Marvin Attack), confirmed via crates.io's own version history, not assumed.

cryptography_libs.name: ed25519-dalek

version.notes: MSRV 1.41, confirmed via GitHub issue discussion. Resolves to latest 1.x.
MSRV 1.60.0 (confirmed via GitHub issue #194) -- genuine breaking API rewrite (signing-context/verify changes) from 1.x, a real multi-era candidate confirmed via crates.io version history.

cryptography_libs.notes: Pure-Rust Ed25519 signing implementation with a confirmed, real 1.x->2.x API break -- policy since 2.x is that any MSRV bump gets its own minor version bump.

cryptography_libs.name: sodiumoxide

version.notes: MSRV >=1.36.0 (needs mem::MaybeUninit), confirmed via sodiumoxide's own README. Resolves to 0.2.7, the FINAL release -- project was formally deprecated/archived on GitHub 2022-09-04 (confirmed), no successor crate named. Needs libsodium-dev apt dependency at build time, same pattern as PyNaCl's runtime libsodium23 dependency elsewhere in this project.

cryptography_libs.notes: Bindings to libsodium (not pure Rust). Deliberately tracked as a real, dated "deprecated but not insecure" library -- distinct from rust-crypto's genuinely-insecure legacy status below.

cryptography_libs.name: bcrypt

version.notes: Confirmed release date via crates.io. Historical MSRV not independently confirmed for this version -- floor is a conservative era-based estimate, verify via real build.
Confirmed release date via crates.io. Historical MSRV not independently confirmed -- floor is an era-based estimate, verify via real build.

Confirmed release date via crates.io. Only the CURRENT (2026) release (0.19.2) has a confirmed rust-version field (1.85, suspiciously recent/high -- flagged as likely only true of the latest patch, not this bucket) -- 0.15's own floor is an era-based estimate, verify via real build.

cryptography_libs.notes: Pure-Rust bcrypt implementation (Keats/rust-bcrypt), no true "majors" -- a continuously incrementing 0.x line since 2015. Three representative points chosen for density rather than genuine API-era boundaries (this library's API has stayed relatively stable across these).

cryptography_libs.name: argon2

version.notes: Confirmed release date via crates.io.
Confirmed release date via crates.io.
MSRV 1.65 confirmed via crates.io rust-version field (RustCrypto's "MSRV bump = minor bump" policy means this applies from roughly this point forward).

cryptography_libs.notes: RustCrypto pure-Rust Argon2 implementation. Three points across its real version history (2021-2023) for density.

cryptography_libs.name: rust-crypto

version.notes: Resolves to 0.2.36, the FINAL release -- confirmed genuinely abandoned (zero commits since 2016), RUSTSEC-2016-0005 unmaintained. The crate's OWN README carries a warning: "has not been thoroughly audited for correctness, so any use where security is important is not recommended." No formal MSRV concept existed yet in 2016 -- this is this registry's strongest "legacy/insecure but historically real" entry, directly parallel to old pycryptodome/phpseclib versions tracked elsewhere. Compatibility is the full range as an OPTIMISTIC estimate -- whether 2016-era pure-Rust code still compiles unmodified on rustc 1.97 has NOT been verified with a real build and may well fail (language/edition changes over a decade are a real risk); treat this as the single most likely combo in the whole registry to need a real-build fix.

cryptography_libs.notes: The original (DaGenix) rust-crypto crate, fully superseded by the modern RustCrypto organization's crates (rsa/aes-gcm/sha2/etc. above) but never formally pointed users there in its own README. Deliberately kept for the same real-CVE-history research angle this project already applies elsewhere.

cryptography_libs.name: openssl

version.notes: Resolves to latest 0.10.x. Bindings to system OpenSSL (sfackler/rust-openssl) -- confirmed via crates.io this crate has been on a single climbing 0.10.x line since ~2016, NOT a multi-era crate like the others in this registry (functionally analogous to this project's Python pyOpenSSL entry -- one long line rather than distinct majors). MSRV has climbed over time: confirmed 1.63.0 at 0.10.73, confirmed 1.80.0 at 0.10.76+ -- the 1.63 floor used here is a compromise for whatever mid-range patch this bucket resolves to; the true correct floor for 'latest' may be higher (1.80), verify via real build. Default (non-vendored) build needs libssl-dev + pkg-config; the 'vendored' feature (via openssl-src) statically builds OpenSSL from source instead, needing a C compiler + perl + make but no libssl-dev -- mirrors this project's Python pyOpenSSL-vs-cryptography build-dependency distinction.

cryptography_libs.notes: System-OpenSSL bindings (the 'raw' angle), included specifically to parallel Python's pyOpenSSL entry in this registry -- deliberately a single bucket given its real one-line version history, not a gap in this registry's density.
